---
title: "Non-Motorized Travel Sensitivity"
subtitle: "WF-TDM Non-Motorized Enhancement"
metadata-files:
  - _title_metadata.yml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
    footer: "Wasatch Front Regional Council | Non-Motorized Sensitivity Analysis"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: july2025
---

```{python}
#| label: setup

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import os
import sys

# All data loading/aggregation lives in run_sets/non-motorized-2023/report_loader.py,
# shared with summary.qmd. It reads test scenarios 1-13 from a frozen snapshot
# once this run set is retired (tdmruns snapshot-run-set / purge-run-set-outputs),
# falling back to whatever tdmruns import-manual-run(-set) most recently curated
# under runs/ otherwise. The base year and TAZ/district shapefiles are always
# read live from the (gitignored) tdm/ working tree -- see CLAUDE.md.
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join('..', '..', '..', 'run_sets', 'non-motorized-2023'))
import report_loader as loader

frames = loader.load()
raw_df = frames['raw_df']
filt_df = raw_df
full_delta = frames['full_delta']
geo = frames['geo_centerization']

purposes = loader.PURPOSES
label_order = loader.LABEL_ORDER
series_label = loader.series_label

COLORS = {'HH only': '#1f77b4', 'EMP only': '#e6550d', 'HH + EMP': '#31a354'}
```

## Why This Matters

:::  {.incremental}
- The WF-TDM has been updated with an enhanced **non-motorized (NM) travel component**
- Before using it in planning applications, we need to know: **does the model respond to land use changes in a credible way?**
- We ran 13 controlled tests — artificially increasing household and employment density in five test districts — to see how walk and bike trips respond
:::

::: {.notes}
The enhancement improves how the model estimates walking and biking. These sensitivity tests are a standard model validation step — we're stress-testing the model's behavior before applying it to real planning scenarios.
:::

## How We Tested {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Test geography:** 5 small districts (278 TAZs) across the region

**What we changed:**

| Group | Scenarios |
|---|---|
| Households only | 2×, 4×, 12× |
| Employment only | 2×, 4×, 12× |
| Both HH + EMP | 2×, 4×, 12× |
| 2050 land use (local) | Base & Centerized |
| 2050 land use (region) | Base & Centerized |

**What we measured:** Non-motorized mode share — the share of daily productions walked or biked
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Why such extreme multipliers?
The 2×, 4×, and 12× values are intentionally unrealistic. They stress-test the model across a wide range of densities so we can understand its behavior — not to represent actual planning scenarios.
:::
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
The test districts were selected to represent a range of urban contexts. The base year is 2019. All results shown are daily trip productions at the non-motorized mode share level.
:::

## Overall Sensitivity {.smaller}

```{python}
#| fig-align: center

mult_all = full_delta[
    (full_delta['test_id'].isin(range(10))) &
    (full_delta['geo_scope'] == 'smldst') &
    (full_delta['Purpose'] == 'All')
].copy()

mult_all['hh_mult_n']  = mult_all['hh_mult'].astype(float)
mult_all['emp_mult_n'] = mult_all['emp_mult'].astype(float)
mult_all['multiplier'] = mult_all[['hh_mult_n', 'emp_mult_n']].max(axis=1)

base_row = mult_all[mult_all['test_id'] == 0].copy()
frames = []
for lbl, mask in [
    ('HH only',  (mult_all['hh_mult_n'] > 1) & (mult_all['emp_mult_n'] == 1)),
    ('EMP only', (mult_all['hh_mult_n'] == 1) & (mult_all['emp_mult_n'] > 1)),
    ('HH + EMP', (mult_all['hh_mult_n'] > 1) & (mult_all['emp_mult_n'] > 1)),
]:
    b = base_row.copy(); b['series'] = lbl
    s = mult_all[mask].copy(); s['series'] = lbl
    frames.extend([b, s])

curve_df = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True).sort_values(['series', 'multiplier'])

fig = px.line(
    curve_df, x='multiplier', y='nonm_share', color='series', markers=True,
    labels={'multiplier': 'Density Multiplier (×)', 'nonm_share': 'Non-Motorized Mode Share (%)', 'series': ''},
    category_orders={'series': ['HH only', 'EMP only', 'HH + EMP']},
    color_discrete_map=COLORS,
)
fig.update_traces(mode='lines+markers', marker_size=10, line_width=3)
fig.update_xaxes(tickvals=[1, 2, 4, 12], tickfont_size=13, title_font_size=14)
fig.update_yaxes(tickfont_size=13, title_font_size=14)
fig.update_layout(
    height=430, width=1100, margin=dict(t=20, b=20, l=20, r=20),
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.02, xanchor='left', x=0, font_size=13),
)
fig.show()
```

*All trip purposes combined | Modified small districts | Daily productions*

::: {.notes}
Base Non-Motorized mode share in the test districts is 15.2%. At 12× employment, the model reaches 49.3%. At 12× households, it reaches 28.0%. The response is non-linear with diminishing returns — the slope is steep at low multipliers and flattens at higher ones.
:::

## Employment Density is the Stronger Driver {.smaller background-color="#f0f5fb"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### At 4× density

| Scenario | NM Share | vs. Base |
|---|---|---|
| Base 2019 | 15.2% | — |
| HH only ×4 | 18.1% | +2.9 pct pts |
| **EMP only ×4** | **29.8%** | **+14.6 pct pts** |
| HH + EMP ×4 | 27.9% | +12.7 pct pts |
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### At 12× density

| Scenario | NM Share | vs. Base |
|---|---|---|
| Base 2019 | 15.2% | — |
| HH only ×12 | 28.0% | +12.8 pct pts |
| **EMP only ×12** | **49.3%** | **+34.1 pct pts** |
| HH + EMP ×12 | 47.4% | +32.3 pct pts |
:::
::::

::: {.callout-important}
At equal multipliers, employment density produces roughly **2–2.5× more non-motorized mode share gain** than household density alone.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is one of the most important findings. Employment density creates walkable destinations — offices, shops, restaurants — that draw short walking trips. Household density increases origin-side density but doesn't create as many walkable destinations.
:::

## Sensitivity by Trip Purpose {.smaller}

```{python}
#| fig-align: center

mult_purp = full_delta[
    (full_delta['test_id'].isin(range(10))) &
    (full_delta['geo_scope'] == 'smldst') &
    (full_delta['Purpose'].isin(purposes))
].copy()

mult_purp['hh_mult_n']  = mult_purp['hh_mult'].astype(float)
mult_purp['emp_mult_n'] = mult_purp['emp_mult'].astype(float)
mult_purp['multiplier'] = mult_purp[['hh_mult_n', 'emp_mult_n']].max(axis=1)

base_purp = mult_purp[mult_purp['test_id'] == 0].copy()
frames2 = []
for lbl, mask in [
    ('HH only',  (mult_purp['hh_mult_n'] > 1) & (mult_purp['emp_mult_n'] == 1)),
    ('EMP only', (mult_purp['hh_mult_n'] == 1) & (mult_purp['emp_mult_n'] > 1)),
    ('HH + EMP', (mult_purp['hh_mult_n'] > 1) & (mult_purp['emp_mult_n'] > 1)),
]:
    b = base_purp.copy(); b['series'] = lbl
    s = mult_purp[mask].copy(); s['series'] = lbl
    frames2.extend([b, s])

curve_purp = pd.concat(frames2, ignore_index=True).sort_values(['Purpose', 'series', 'multiplier'])
purpose_labels = {'HBW': 'Home–Based–Work', 'HBO': 'Home-Based-Other', 'NHB': 'Non-Home–Based'}
curve_purp['Purpose Label'] = curve_purp['Purpose'].map(purpose_labels)

fig = px.line(
    curve_purp, x='multiplier', y='nonm_share', color='series',
    facet_col='Purpose Label', markers=True,
    labels={'multiplier': 'Density Multiplier (×)', 'nonm_share': 'Non-Motorized Mode Share (%)', 'series': ''},
    category_orders={
        'series': ['HH only', 'EMP only', 'HH + EMP'],
        'Purpose Label': [purpose_labels[p] for p in purposes],
    },
    color_discrete_map=COLORS,
)
fig.update_traces(mode='lines+markers', marker_size=8, line_width=2.5)
fig.update_xaxes(tickvals=[1, 2, 4, 12], tickfont_size=12)
fig.update_yaxes(tickfont_size=12)
fig.for_each_annotation(lambda a: a.update(text=a.text.split('=')[-1], font_size=14))
fig.update_layout(
    height=400, width=1100, margin=dict(t=40, b=20, l=20, r=20),
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.08, xanchor='left', x=0, font_size=12),
)
fig.show()
```

**Home-Based-Other** and **Non-Home–Based** trips are the most sensitive to employment density — these include lunch, errands, and midday travel that become walkable in dense employment environments.

::: {.notes}
Home–Based–Work trips respond more modestly and more evenly — commute trips have longer distances and are more auto-dependent even in dense areas. Home-Based-Other and NHB trips are short by nature, so walkability improvements have an outsized effect.
:::

## Walk vs. Bike: Almost All Gains are Walking {.smaller}

```{python}
#| fig-align: center

wb = full_delta[
    (full_delta['test_id'].isin(range(1, 10))) &
    (full_delta['geo_scope'] == 'smldst') &
    (full_delta['Purpose'] == 'All')
].copy()

wb['hh_mult_n']  = wb['hh_mult'].astype(float)
wb['emp_mult_n'] = wb['emp_mult'].astype(float)
wb['series']     = wb.apply(series_label, axis=1)
wb['multiplier'] = wb[['hh_mult_n', 'emp_mult_n']].max(axis=1).astype(int)
wb['label']      = wb['series'] + ' ' + wb['multiplier'].astype(str) + '×'

wb_melt = wb.melt(
    id_vars=['label'],
    value_vars=['delta_walk_share', 'delta_bike_share'],
    var_name='mode', value_name='delta_share'
)
wb_melt['mode'] = wb_melt['mode'].map({'delta_walk_share': 'Walk', 'delta_bike_share': 'Bike'})

fig = px.bar(
    wb_melt, x='label', y='delta_share', color='mode', barmode='stack',
    labels={'label': '', 'delta_share': 'Change in Mode Share (pct pts)', 'mode': ''},
    category_orders={
        'label': [l.replace('x', '×') for l in label_order],
        'mode': ['Walk', 'Bike']
    },
    color_discrete_map={'Walk': '#2196F3', 'Bike': '#FF9800'},
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=390, width=1100, margin=dict(t=20, b=20, l=20, r=20),
    xaxis_tickangle=-30, xaxis_tickfont_size=12,
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.02, xanchor='left', x=0, font_size=13),
)
fig.show()
```

EMP scenarios: walk gains are 10–100× larger than bike gains. &nbsp; HH scenarios: both modes increase, but walk still dominates.

::: {.notes}
For EMP x12: walk share rises ~36 percentage points, bike share rises ~0.3 percentage points. Employment density creates short-distance walkable trips, not cycling trips. Household density scenarios show more balanced gains — residential density boosts both walking and biking.
:::

## 2050 Forecasts in Context {.smaller}

```{python}
#| fig-align: center

future_ids = [0, 10, 11, 12, 13]
future = full_delta[
    full_delta['test_id'].isin(future_ids) &
    (full_delta['Purpose'] == 'All')
].copy()

future['geo_label'] = future['geo_scope'].map({
    'smldst': 'Local (Test Districts)',
    'region': 'Region-Wide'
})

desc_order = ['Base 2019', 'Future Today', 'Future Today - Centerized']

fig = px.bar(
    future, x='description', y='nonm_share', color='geo_label', barmode='group',
    text=future['nonm_share'].round(1).astype(str) + '%',
    labels={'description': '', 'nonm_share': 'Non-Motorized Mode Share (%)', 'geo_label': ''},
    category_orders={
        'description': desc_order,
        'geo_label': ['Local (Test Districts)', 'Region-Wide'],
    },
    color_discrete_map={'Local (Test Districts)': '#1f77b4', 'Region-Wide': '#d62728'},
)
fig.update_traces(textposition='outside', textfont_size=13)
fig.update_layout(
    height=400, width=1100, margin=dict(t=30, b=20, l=20, r=20),
    yaxis_range=[0, 25], xaxis_tickfont_size=13,
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.02, xanchor='left', x=0, font_size=13),
)
fig.show()
```

2050 local forecasts land at **~19%** — comparable to a **2× multiplier** — a moderate, credible response. Centerized appears identical at this local scale because the focal TAZs sit in areas little affected by the redistribution. Region-wide, centerization adds ~7,000 more non-motorized trips (+0.07 percentage points).

::: {.notes}
The focal test-district TAZs (tests 0–11) happen to sit in areas not significantly affected by the centerization redistribution — that's why Centerized appears nearly identical here. The real signal is at the district level, shown on the next slide.
:::

## Where Centerization Has an Effect {.smaller}

```{python}
#| fig-align: center

# geo (district-level share/density-change table) is built by
# report_loader.build_geo_centerization() and already loaded in the setup cell.

fig = px.scatter(
    geo, x='dens_diff', y='delta_share', color='direction', text='label',
    hover_name='DMED_NAME',
    hover_data={'dens_diff': ':.1f', 'delta_share': ':.2f', 'direction': False},
    labels={
        'dens_diff':   'HH Density Change (HH/acre, Centerized vs Future Today)',
        'delta_share': 'Change in Non-Motorized Mode Share (pct pts)',
        'direction':   '',
    },
    color_discrete_map={'Gaining density': '#2196F3', 'Losing density': '#e6550d'},
)
fig.update_traces(marker_size=10, textposition='top center', textfont_size=11)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash='dash', line_color='gray', line_width=1)
fig.add_vline(x=0, line_dash='dash', line_color='gray', line_width=1)
fig.update_layout(
    height=390, width=1100, margin=dict(t=20, b=20, l=20, r=20),
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.02, xanchor='left', x=0, font_size=13),
    xaxis_title_font_size=13, yaxis_title_font_size=13,
)
fig.show()
```

Districts **gaining** households (blue) → Non-Motorized share rises. &nbsp; Districts **losing** households (orange) → Non-Motorized share falls.

::: {.notes}
Ogden Core gains +3.0 percentage points with 13,500 more households — the strongest single-district signal. North Ogden loses 0.5 percentage points with 6,600 fewer. The aggregate test-district comparison looked flat because those 4 focal TAZs happened to sit in areas little affected by the redistribution. At the district and region-wide level, the model correctly captures the effect: denser areas get more walking and biking.
:::

## Non-Motorized (NM) Key Takeaways

::: {.incremental}
1. **The model passes the test** — NM mode share increases consistently and proportionally with density increases in the expected direction.

2. **Employment density is the primary lever** — at equal multipliers, EMP produces 2–2.5× more NM mode share gain than households.

3. **Walk trips, not bike, drive the gains** — especially in high-employment scenarios.

4. **Home-Based-Other and Non-Home–Based trips are most sensitive** — short-trip purposes respond most to walkable environments.

5. **2050 forecasts are in a reasonable range** — comparable to roughly 2× current density, not extreme.
:::

::: {.notes}
Final message: The model is behaving as expected. These results support using the enhanced non-motorized component in planning applications. The full technical report with interactive charts is available as a companion document.
:::

## Summary

<br><br>

[View a more detailed summary](summary.html){style="font-size: 1.2em; color: #3498db; font-weight: bold; text-decoration: none; border: 2px solid #3498db; padding: 0.4em 0.8em; border-radius: 8px;"}
